section: Chapter: Parliament and the Making of Laws

questions.question: What is Parliament?
questions.correct_answer: Parliament is the highest law-making body of India.
questions.explanation: It represents the people of the country and makes laws for the nation.

questions.question: What are the two houses of the Indian Parliament?
questions.explanation: Lok Sabha represents the people while Rajya Sabha represents the states.

questions.question: Who are Members of Parliament (MPs)?
questions.correct_answer: Representatives elected by people to represent their constituencies in Parliament.
questions.explanation: MPs raise issues of citizens and participate in law-making.

questions.question: What is the role of Parliament in a democracy?
questions.correct_answer: To make laws, represent the people and control the government.
questions.explanation: Parliament ensures that government actions follow democratic principles.

questions.question: What is a bill?
questions.correct_answer: A proposal for a new law or change in an existing law.
questions.explanation: A bill becomes a law after being approved by Parliament.

questions.question: What is Question Hour?
questions.correct_answer: The first hour of a parliamentary session when MPs ask questions to ministers.
questions.explanation: It helps ensure accountability of the government.

questions.question: What is the role of the opposition in Parliament?
questions.correct_answer: To question and challenge the actions of the government.
questions.explanation: The opposition helps ensure that the government works responsibly.

questions.question: Why are debates important in Parliament?
questions.correct_answer: They allow members to discuss and evaluate different viewpoints before making decisions.
questions.explanation: Debates help in making well-informed laws.

questions.question: How do public issues become laws?
questions.correct_answer: Public concerns are raised by MPs and discussed in Parliament, which may lead to new laws.

questions.question: Who gives the final approval for a bill to become a law?
questions.explanation: After approval by both houses of Parliament, the bill is sent to the President for assent.

notes.title: Structure of Parliament
notes.content: The Indian Parliament consists of two houses: Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha.

notes.title: Law-Making Process
notes.content: A proposal called a bill is discussed in Parliament and becomes a law after approval by both houses and the President.

notes.title: Role of Opposition
notes.content: Opposition parties question the government and ensure accountability.

notes.title: Question Hour
notes.content: During Question Hour, MPs ask ministers about government activities and decisions.

notes.title: Public Participation
notes.content: Citizens influence law-making by raising issues and concerns through their representatives.

facts.fact: Lok Sabha is called the House of the People.
Rajya Sabha is called the Council of States.
Members of Parliament represent the interests of their constituencies.
Debates and discussions are essential for making democratic laws.
